conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.

That's a long history with bidis, and it's great that you're taking some steps to reduce your smoking due to the doctor's advice. What's the most challenging part about cutting down on your bidis, is it when you're feeling stressed or in a specific situation, or something else?
Waking up in the morning is the hardest for me. That's when I usually have one first. Stopping it then is toughest.

It sounds like morning is a really tough time for you to resist smoking, especially since it's become a habit to have that first bidi as soon as you wake up. Have you ever tried doing something different immediately after waking up, like drinking a full glass of water, to see if it helps?
Drinking water... yeah, I do that sometimes. But it doesn't really help me not think about smoking.

It's okay that drinking water hasn't helped you as much as you'd like, it sounds like you still need something more to help you shake off the morning craving. Can you think of a simple activity that you've enjoyed in the past, something that might take your mind off smoking for a few minutes?
Walking around the fields before work, yes. I like that. It's calming and gets me moving.

It sounds like you really enjoy walking in the fields - it's a great way to calm down and get moving. Would you be willing to try incorporating a short walk into your morning routine, maybe even just 5-10 minutes, to see if it can help reduce your cravings for bidis?
I can try. It's doable. But what if the cravings come back while I'm walking?

You're considering what to do if cravings come up while you're walking. Have you ever talked to someone about your cravings when you're feeling overwhelmed, like a family member or friend, to see if that might help you feel better and reduce the urge to smoke?
My brother-in-law, he's always asking me how I am, and I tell him sometimes. But I don't really talk about the smoking, it's not something I want to burden him with.

It sounds like you feel like you could trust your brother-in-law with your struggles, but you're hesitant to burden him with the details. Can you imagine telling him specifically about the times you feel overwhelmed and crave a bidi, and how talking about it might help you feel supported and less alone?
Maybe... I don't know. I just don't want to make him worry.

It sounds like you're concerned about your brother-in-law's reaction and not wanting to worry him. Can you imagine how it might feel for him to know that you're struggling with your cravings and that he could be a source of support for you?
I suppose he might be understanding... yeah, that's right.
It sounds like you're starting to consider reaching out to your brother-in-law for support. Would you be willing to try sharing your struggles with him and seeing how it goes?
I can try. Next time I see him, I'll think about telling him.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit more open to sharing your struggles with your brother-in-law. Can you imagine practicing a simple phrase with me to say no to a bidi when someone offers it to you, just in case an opportunity arises before you get a chance to talk to him?
No, I don't want a bidi now.

You're taking a step in the right direction by being able to say no to a bidi when offered. Can you imagine keeping a small journal to track when you feel the urge to smoke a bidi, so we can identify any patterns and come up with a plan to address them together?
I can do that, yeah. Write down when I crave it and how I felt.
You're feeling more confident about keeping a journal to track your cravings. What do you think would make it easier for you to write down your feelings and triggers in that journal each day?
If you can give me a small notebook, one that's easy to carry... that would help.
I'd be happy to get you that small notebook.  Would you be willing to try carrying it with you at all times, even when you're not expecting to have a craving?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.

You've been smoking bidis for a long time, and it's become a part of your daily routine. What do you usually do right before you smoke a bidi, is there a specific time of day or activity that triggers your craving?
It happens most when I'm working on site, my coworkers smoke and offer me one. They say, "Have one, yaar," and it becomes easy to give in.
It sounds like your coworkers' invitations can be quite tempting. How do you feel about quitting smoking, is there a specific reason or goal that's driving you to want to quit now?

To be honest, my wife's been on my case lately about it. She wants to save money, and it's true, I spend a lot on bidis, around 300 rupees a month. And I guess it's also true that it's not good for me, but...

It sounds like your wife's concerns about the cost of bidis and your health are really resonating with you. Would you be open to trying something to help distract you from cravings and keep your hands busy when you feel the urge to smoke at work?

I don't know... I mean, I've tried quitting before, but it didn't work. And it's not like I can just stop because I want to, I need to find a way to deal with the situation on site. My coworkers smoke all the time, it's not like I can just sit there doing nothing.

It sounds like you're struggling with feeling like you need to quit right now, in the moment, because of your coworkers. Have you ever thought about practicing a simple way to politely say no to their offers, like telling them you're just taking a break from smoking for today?
Yeah, okay... I can try saying that. But what if they ask why, and I have to say "nothing"? It feels like a lie.

It sounds like saying "I'm just taking a break from smoking for today" feels like a temporary solution rather than a truthful answer. What do you think would happen if you actually told your coworker the real reason you're quitting, like your wife's concerns and your own health worries?
I don't know... I don't want to be that guy on the site who complains about his wife's nagging. They'd probably just tease me or tell me to sort out my own life, you know?

It sounds like you're worried about how your coworkers might react if you share your reasons for quitting. 

Are you comfortable with the idea of telling a close friend or family member about your struggles with quitting, and having that person to support and remind you of your reasons for quitting?

My brother's actually been bugging me to quit for a while. He's not a smoker, and he's always saying I'm being crazy for smoking so much. Maybe I could tell him I'm trying to quit, and he could be some support. But what if he just says "I told you so"?

That's a good feeling to have your brother's support in mind. Does the thought of him saying "I told you so" scare you away from reaching out to him for help, or can you imagine how having his support might motivate you to keep going with your quit plan?
I guess... having his support might actually help me stay on track. It's not like I've got anyone else to turn to, and if he's on my side, it might make a difference.
It sounds like having your brother's support could be a game-changer for you. Would you be willing to reach out to him, share your struggles with quitting, and ask for his help in staying on track?
Yeah, okay... I think I can try calling him later today. Maybe we can even meet up for a cup of chai and talk about it in person.
That's a great first step in reaching out to your brother for support. What do you think you'll say to him when you talk about your struggles with quitting, and how do you imagine he might respond?

I'll just be honest with him, tell him how I feel about quitting and why I'm struggling. I don't know... maybe he'll just tell me to be strong and quit already, but maybe he'll actually listen and offer some advice or help.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about how your brother will respond. What are some things you've learned from your past attempts at quitting that you could share with him to help him better understand your struggles?

I've tried quitting a couple of times before, but I never really had a plan. I just stopped smoking for a few days and then went back to it. This time, I think I need a better plan, and maybe my brother can help me come up with one.

It sounds like you're recognizing the importance of having a plan to help you quit this time around. What if we were to try a simple plan where you avoid situations at work where smoking is common, for the first week, and instead take short breaks in a different area to help manage your stress and cravings?
That might actually work. I can try sitting in my truck during breaks instead of hanging out with the guys who smoke. It's a small thing, but it might make a difference.

You're willing to try a new approach to manage your stress and cravings at work. How do you think you'll feel about not being able to smoke during your breaks, and are you concerned that it might affect your relationships with your coworkers?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.

It sounds like you've been smoking bidis for a pretty long time, and you've actually been able to cut back a bit recently. What do you usually do when you feel like reaching for a bidi - is it more about relaxation, socializing, or something else?
Honestly, it's when I wake up, especially if I'm lying awake. It helps calm me down a bit, I guess.

It sounds like you're using bidis as a way to calm down and relax your mind when you wake up. What is it about being awake at night that makes you feel the need for something to calm you down, do you think?
It's been... two years now, since my wife passed away. I wake up in the middle of the night, and I miss her. It's hard to fall back asleep when you're alone in the house, you know?

It sounds like losing your wife has had a profound impact on you, and it's affecting your sleep as well as your desire to smoke bidis. What do you think would be the most important thing for you to achieve by quitting smoking bidis, something that would make it worth the effort for you?
My daughter brought me here. She says it's not good for my health, and I believe her. I want to be healthy, for her. And maybe it'll help me sleep better, too.

It sounds like your daughter's concern has motivated you to want to be healthy for her. Can you think of any other activities that you could do first thing in the morning, instead of smoking a bidi, that might help you feel more calm and relaxed, like maybe going for a short walk or doing some light stretching?
I used to like reading, but I don't do that much anymore. Maybe I could start reading again. It used to calm me down, you know?

It sounds like reading might be a good way to calm yourself down in the morning. Would you be willing to try setting aside a few minutes each morning to read something you find relaxing, like a book or a magazine, and see if that helps reduce your urge to smoke a bidi?
I'm not sure... it's hard to get started, you know? But maybe I can try. I don't want to get up too early, though. That's when I usually have a bidi.

You're feeling uncertain about getting started with reading in the morning. Can you think of a small, manageable change you could make to start your day, like getting dressed or making a cup of tea, that you could do before reading, so it feels less daunting?
Making a cup of tea... that's easy. I can do that. But it's not just the tea, it's the bidi afterwards. That's what's hard.
You feel really stuck on the bidi craving after tea. Can you think of drinking a full glass of water after you make your tea, instead of smoking a bidi, and see how that feels?
Drinking water... that's a good idea, I guess. It's better than nothing, but I'm still not sure.
You think drinking water is a good idea, but you're still unsure about it. Can you imagine how it would feel after a few days of drinking water after tea, instead of smoking a bidi?
It might help, I suppose. I don't know, it's still hard to think about. But maybe it's a start.
You're feeling a bit uncertain about this idea, but you're also open to trying it. Would you be willing to give drinking water after tea a try for the next few days, just to see how it goes?
Yeah, okay. I'll try it. But it's not like I'm promising anything.
I'm so glad you're taking this step forward with an open mind – take care of yourself as you explore this new approach.
Thank you. I'll try.
May you find peace and clarity as you move forward into this new chapter.
I would like that. For my daughter's sake.

You're thinking about your daughter, which brings up your motivation to quit - that's really beautiful. 

Are you finding that thinking about your daughter's future is a strong motivator for you to stay smoke-free?
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
I'm glad you decided to seek help for yourself, it takes a lot of courage to do so. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
About 10, maybe a bit more when things get really tough.

It sounds like you have a pretty strong nicotine habit, considering you smoke around 10 to 15 cigarettes a day. How do you think your life would change for the better if you were able to quit smoking?
My wife says it would be healthier, but I'm not so sure. I mean, it helps me calm down when I'm angry or stressed. What else can I do?

It sounds like you're trying to find alternative ways to cope with stress and anger, and you're not sure what else you can do to calm yourself down. Have you ever noticed how you feel after taking a short walk outside when you're feeling stressed?
I go for walks, yeah. It helps a bit, but it's not the same as smoking. I just seem to feel better after a cigarette, you know?

You mentioned going for walks helped a bit, but it's not the same as smoking. That got me curious, have you ever tried chewing gum or eating healthy snacks when you feel like reaching for a cigarette, just to see if it helps distract you?
I do chew gum sometimes, but it doesn't do much. My mind's already made up to have a cigarette. I don't know, it's just a habit, I guess.

You've tried chewing gum, but it didn't help break your cigarette habit, and you think it's just a matter of willpower and habit. Have you ever tried chewing gum in a different way, like chewing it slowly and then placing it between your cheek and gum to see if that makes a difference?

No, I just chew it and then spit it out. I don't know about that other way. Look, can we talk about something else? You're always asking about the gum and walks, what about my anger problem? Why do I keep smoking when I'm angry, that's the real question.

You're feeling frustrated with our conversation turning back to the gum and walks, and you're really wanting to explore your anger problem. Can you tell me what you think might happen if you were able to manage your anger differently, without reaching for a cigarette?
I think I would be more in control, I guess. I wouldn't yell at the kids, and I wouldn't take things out on my wife. But how do I do that? I don't know, I feel like a mess when I get angry.

You mentioned managing your anger differently, and not yelling at your kids or taking things out on your wife would be a huge relief. Can you think of a situation where you felt less angry, and maybe more in control, and what triggered that feeling?

Well, there was a time when my wife and I were at a school play with our son, and we had a disagreement about how to handle a situation with the teacher. But instead of smoking, I just took some deep breaths and walked around the block. When I came back, I felt better. But that was just one time, and I don't know how to make that happen all the time.

You remember that moment at the school play when deep breathing and taking a short walk helped you feel better, and you're wondering how to make that happen more often. Can you imagine yourself walking around the block or taking a few deep breaths in the moment when you feel yourself getting angry, like right now?
Yeah, I guess I can imagine it, but it's not the same as actually doing it. And it's hard to control when I get angry, you know? It just happens.

It sounds like you're struggling to connect the imagined feeling of control with the actual experience of getting angry. Can you think of someone close to you who you admire for their ability to stay calm in difficult situations, and what you think might be helping them stay calm that you could try for yourself?

My wife is pretty calm, even when we're arguing. I don't know, she just seems to take a step back and think before we talk. I've seen her do that when our son's behavior is getting on her nerves too. But I don't know if it's just because she's more patient than me or what.

It sounds like you really admire your wife's calmness, especially in difficult situations, and you're wondering what might be helping her stay calm that you could try for yourself. Do you think taking a step back and thinking before reacting might be a strategy she uses that you could try to adopt in your own life?
Maybe. Yeah, that's probably it. She just takes a pause before responding. And it helps us communicate better, I guess.

You think taking a pause before reacting might be a helpful strategy for you, similar to what your wife does. Can you imagine how that pause might feel in your body, like a pause in your breathing or a moment of stillness, when you start feeling angry or triggered to chew tobacco?
It would be... quiet, I guess. Like my breath stops for a second. I think I might be able to think more clearly if I do that.

It sounds like taking a pause and noticing a quiet moment in your body could be a helpful strategy for you. Would you be willing to try a brief pause, like a minute or two, before reacting to your anger or cravings, to see if it helps you feel more in control?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

You seem to feel quite confident about your smoking habit, and it's not uncommon to downplay the risks when it's a long-standing part of our lives. What is it that makes you think you're "fine" with it, considering you've been smoking for 25 years?

My wife is always on my case about it, but I don't feel like it's affecting me that much. I can still do my job, I don't get as tired as I used to, and I've always been active, playing cricket in my youth.

You feel like your wife's concerns aren't really impacting you, but that's also a reminder that she cares enough to bring it up. Are you worried that giving up smoking might change how she sees you, or affect your relationship in some way?

I suppose I'd rather not have her nagging me about it anymore, to be honest. She's always on my case about something, and this feels like one of those things where she's trying to tell me how to live my life.
That makes sense, feeling like your wife is trying to control you can be frustrating. Do you think there's a part of you that secretly appreciates her concern, even if you don't want to admit it?

I don't know... maybe. She's been my wife for a long time, and she knows me. I guess deep down I do care about her, and if she's worried, maybe that means something. But I'm not ready to go talk about this with her yet, I need to figure some things out for myself.

You seem to be acknowledging that there's a part of you that cares about your wife's concern, even if you're not ready to act on it yet. What are some specific situations where you feel like your wife's nagging about your smoking is most frustrating for you?

It's when we're just sitting at home after tea, having a quiet morning, and she starts bringing it up again. Or when I'm stressed out because of the shop, and she tells me I should quit smoking to calm down. It feels like she's not listening to me, like she's just trying to fix me.

Those situations sound like two of your triggers, after tea at home and work stress. Can you imagine a way to change the routine of having a cigarette after tea, without getting rid of the comfort of that quiet morning together?

I guess... we could do something else together after tea. Maybe take a short walk or listen to some music. That way, I'm still relaxing with her, but I'm not reaching for a cigarette. It seems okay, I suppose.

You're open to trying something different after tea, that's a great start. How do you think you could make taking a short walk or listening to music a more automatic habit, so that it feels as natural as reaching for a cigarette after eating?

I think if we made it a routine to walk or listen to music after tea, it would become a habit. Maybe we could even start doing it on our own before she reminds me to quit smoking. That way, it would feel like something we do together, but also like something I'm doing for myself.

That's an interesting idea, incorporating your own initiative into making the walk or listening to music a habit. How do you think you could make that first step, starting the new routine without waiting for your wife to remind you, but still involving her in the process?

I could try setting my watch or phone to remind me to go for a walk or put on music after tea, and then I could invite her to join me. That way, it's like we're doing it together, but it's also something I'm doing on my own initiative. It's a small thing, but it might be a start.

It sounds like you've come up with a plan to make walking or listening to music after tea a new habit. How do you think you could make the reminder from your watch or phone a strong enough trigger to actually get you up and moving, rather than just sitting there thinking about it?
I could set the reminder to a specific song or a funny alarm sound, so when it goes off, it's something that actually grabs my attention and makes me want to get up and do something.

That's a creative idea, using a song or funny alarm sound to grab your attention. How do you think you could apply that same creative energy to creating a more meaningful reward for yourself after resisting the urge to smoke?

Maybe I could treat myself to a small reward after a few days or a week without smoking, like a favorite dish or a nice cup of coffee from the new shop downtown. Something that feels like a real privilege, not just a nag from my wife.

You've come up with some creative ideas for rewards and reminders, and it sounds like you're feeling more in control of your quit plan. Can you think of one specific instance, like a recent difficult workday, where you successfully resisted the urge to smoke and would like to reward yourself for that?

There was a day last week when a customer was being super annoying, and I really wanted to take a break and smoke. But I didn't. I took some deep breaths instead and focused on the sale. After the customer left, I felt a sense of accomplishment and pride in myself. That's the kind of moment I'd like to reward myself for.

It sounds like resisting the urge to smoke in that situation gave you a sense of pride and accomplishment. What do you think would be a small but meaningful reward for that moment, something that would help you feel more motivated to keep resisting the urge in the future?

I think I would like to treat myself to a new pen for my shop. A nice pen is something I've been wanting, but I've been putting it off. It would be a small thing, but it would feel like a real reward, and it's something I could use every day as a reminder of that difficult day when I resisted the urge to smoke.

That sounds like a great idea, treating yourself to a new pen. Considering your morning craving trigger, would you be open to trying a small exercise, like drawing or doodling, to keep your hands busy and distract yourself from the urge to smoke?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

I can see that you've been through this before, and it's frustrating when it doesn't seem to work out. What's one thing that has stayed with you from those previous attempts to quit, something that you remember doing or feeling that might give us a clue about what's going on?
The anxiety. It's always the same - I feel like I'm on edge, my stomach's in knots, and I just can't relax. I've been like that for days after I try to quit, and it's just so hard to deal with.

It sounds like the anxiety is really a huge challenge for you, especially after trying to quit. Can you think of a specific time when you managed to relax or feel some sense of calm, even if it was just for a short while, and what helped you do that?

One evening... I was at my sister's house, and we were watching a movie together. We had a big bowl of popcorn, and for some reason, I didn't even think about cigarettes. It was like my mind just forgot. I remember feeling really calm, just enjoying the movie and my sister's company. But it was just one evening, and it didn't last.

You said you felt calm and relaxed at your sister's house, watching a movie with a big bowl of popcorn. That's a great memory, and it gives me hope that it's possible for you to feel that way again. 
Can you think of what specifically about that evening helped you feel so calm and relaxed, was it the company, the activity, or something else?

I think it was just being surrounded by people I love... my sister and her kids. It was a nice, quiet evening, and I wasn't feeling stressed or anxious like I often do at home in the evenings. And yeah, not thinking about cigarettes was a big part of it, too. It felt so... normal.

It sounds like being around loved ones and feeling a sense of normalcy might be really helpful for you. Would you be open to trying a short exercise, where you imagine yourself in a similar situation to that evening at your sister's house, and see if you can recreate some of that calm feeling?
I don't know... I'm not sure I can just "imagine" feeling calm. It's hard to explain, but it feels like the anxiety is just always there, underneath everything. I'm not sure I can just turn it off.
It sounds like the anxiety can feel overwhelming and hard to shake off. Are you more likely to feel anxious when you're alone at home, or is it more present in certain situations or activities?

It's definitely worse when I'm home alone in the evenings. That's when I usually reach for a cigarette, just out of habit, I guess. And it's not just the smoking, it's the whole routine that goes with it... watching TV, browsing my phone... it's just a really comfortable, familiar thing to do when I'm alone, but it feels like it's holding me back from being able to relax, you know?

You're struggling with the familiar routine of watching TV or browsing your phone in the evenings, which seems to be tied to your habit of smoking. Can you think of a way to replace that routine with something else that brings you a sense of calm and relaxation?

I don't know... I've tried reading, but I get bored after a while. And I've tried going for walks, but it's hard to motivate myself to get out the door. I feel like I need something that's just as easy and convenient as watching TV, you know?

It sounds like you've tried a couple of activities that didn't quite fit your needs. Have you ever tried doing some light stretching or yoga while sitting on the couch, which might be a way to relax without having to leave the house?

I have tried it a few times, but I only made it through like, 5 minutes before I got distracted and quit. I don't know... it just felt like too much work to keep myself from getting bored. But I suppose it's worth trying again?

It sounds like the experience of trying light stretching or yoga was somewhat helpful, but getting bored and distracted was a major hurdle. Can you think of a way to make the experience more engaging and enjoyable for yourself, like setting a timer or finding a video that guides you through it?

I could try that. I've seen videos online where they do guided stretching and breathing, and it seems really calming. Maybe if I have someone else telling me what to do, I can stay focused. But what if I get bored with the videos, too? It's hard to commit to something that might not stick.
It sounds like you're still a bit hesitant about committing to a new routine. Have you considered setting a small goal, like watching one guided video per evening, to start with, and see how it goes?

I guess that's a good starting point. One video a night sounds manageable, like a tiny step. Maybe if I can just get through one, I can try again the next night... and the next. Okay, yeah. I can try that.
It sounds like you've found a sense of momentum, and I wish you all the best as you take this small step towards your goals.
It felt good to talk this through, and it's nice to have a little bit of hope again. Thank you for listening.

I'm glad you're feeling hopeful again. Now, let's think about how you can track your progress and stay motivated - have you considered keeping a small journal or log to write down your thoughts and feelings each day after trying the guided video?

I hadn't thought of that, but it might be helpful to see how I'm doing each day. Writing down my thoughts and feelings could help me stay accountable and see how I'm progressing. Okay, I'll try that too.
You're taking a thoughtful step forward, and I wish you all the best on this new path.
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
It sounds like working at a dhaba where smoking is a big part of the culture makes it harder for you to imagine quitting. How long have you been working at the dhaba?
About 10 years now, since I was 24. It's just something I've gotten used to, you know? Everyone smokes during breaks, it's like a way to relax and catch up with each other.

It sounds like working at the dhaba has become a big part of your daily routine, and smoking is closely tied to your breaks and social interactions there. What do you typically do during those breaks when you're not smoking?

Just stand around, chat with the guys, sometimes play some cards or watch TV if we've got a new phone to keep ourselves entertained. It's not exactly exciting, but it's just something we do. You know, pass the time.
It sounds like those breaks are a way to socialize and unwind with your coworkers. What do you think you would miss most about those breaks if you couldn't smoke?

I guess just the camaraderie, hanging out with the guys, sharing a laugh or two. Smoking's just something we do, but if you took that away, I don't know, it'd feel weird, I suppose. Like we're all doing something without doing it.

It sounds like the camaraderie and social aspect of smoking with your coworkers is a significant part of what you'll miss when quitting. What do you think would happen if you found other ways to socialize with your coworkers during breaks?

I don't know... maybe we'd just find something else to do, like play a game or something, but it wouldn't be the same, you know? It's like, smoking's just part of how we interact, it's a shared thing. I'm not sure we'd all just magically start doing something else together.

It sounds like you're worried that quitting smoking might change the dynamic of your interactions with your coworkers. Can you think of a time when you and your coworkers did something together without smoking, and what was it like?

Actually, yeah... there was this one time, we were all out celebrating a friend's birthday, and we went to watch a cricket match together. We didn't smoke at all, we just hung out, ate some snacks, watched the game... it was actually pretty nice, I thought. But, I don't know, that was a special occasion, not just a regular day at the dhaba.

You're remembering a time when you were able to socialize with your coworkers without smoking, and it sounds like it was a positive experience. What was it about that day that made it feel like it wasn't missing the smoking aspect?

I think it was because everyone was in a good mood, you know? It was a celebration, so we were all relaxed and having fun, and the game was pretty interesting, too. We were all just caught up in it, cheering and stuff. I guess what I'm saying is, if we're all having fun, we can find other ways to enjoy each other's company that don't involve smoking.

You're starting to see that it's possible to have a good time with your coworkers without smoking. Have you ever tried suggesting a group activity or a team-building exercise to your coworkers, like going for a short walk or playing a sport together, to see if it could be a fun way to spend time together while quitting smoking?

Actually, no... I never thought of that. I mean, we've talked about playing some cricket or something, but it was always just a joke, never seriously. But, you know, it's not a bad idea. Maybe I'll bring it up next time we're on a break.

It sounds like suggesting a group activity could be a good way to create new, smoke-free memories with your coworkers. Would you be willing to try this and see how it goes the next time you're on a break together?
Yeah, I'll try. It's not a big deal, and if it doesn't work out, we can always go back to smoking. But if it does, it might be something we can all enjoy together. I'm game to try, at least.
I'm so proud of your willingness to take this step and explore new possibilities with your coworkers.
It's not like I'm committed to quitting or anything, but it's just trying something new, you know? Maybe it'll be good, maybe it won't, but at least I'll know I gave it a shot.

You're still a little hesitant about making a commitment to quitting, and that's okay. How do you think it would feel if you were able to share how you're doing with your coworkers, especially if they're supportive of your efforts to quit?

That's a good point, I guess. If they were all supportive, it would actually make it easier for me to stick with it. I could tell them how it's going, and if they're all on the same page, we could figure it out together. That might actually be kinda cool.

You're starting to see the potential benefits of having a supportive crew behind you. Would you be open to having one of your coworkers be the first person you call when you're feeling the urge to smoke in a social situation?

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. One of the guys, Rohan, he's actually a pretty good guy, we've been friends for years. If I'm feeling stuck, maybe I could call him, and he could help me find something else to do. That might actually work.

That's great to hear that you're considering reaching out to Rohan for support. Now, let's think about how you'll approach Rohan about being that person - are you more likely to talk to him about this when you're feeling calm and confident, or when you're in the midst of a strong craving?
I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.

It sounds like that bad week at work really threw you off track, and it's easy to see how challenging it must have been to stay quit under so much stress. Do you think there was anything in particular about that week that made it especially hard for you to resist smoking?
It was the constant meetings and tight deadlines. I felt like I was going to lose my job over it. And my manager was breathing down my neck. That anxiety just took over, I guess.

It sounds like those tight deadlines and meetings really amplified your anxiety, making it feel almost impossible to resist smoking at the time. Do you think it's possible that you might be experiencing some of that anxiety again, even if it's not as extreme as it was during that bad week at work?
Yeah, I still get anxious about deadlines and meetings, it's just part of my job. But I don't know, I feel like I should be able to handle it better now that I've quit before.

You're right, you have had experience quitting before, and that's actually a strength, not a weakness. What did you find was the most challenging part about quitting last time, and do you think you could apply some of what you learned from that experience to this time around?

I guess it was just the feeling of deprivation, you know, not being able to smoke when I wanted to. And the withdrawal symptoms, my headaches were really bad at first. But I was surprised by how much it helped to have a routine, to do things like take long walks after work. It made the cravings feel more manageable.

It sounds like you really connected with having a routine and doing activities like taking long walks to help with cravings last time. Can you imagine incorporating more physical activity into your daily routine, such as taking short walks during your breaks at work, to help you cope with stress and cravings?

Yeah, that could work. I could take short walks during breaks, or maybe even do some stretching exercises at my desk. It's just that when I'm in a meeting, I don't really have a chance to get up and walk around. What do you suggest I do during those times?

You've already found that taking long walks helped, so I'm wondering if you could try taking a few deep breaths and reminding yourself of how you felt after those walks, to see if that can help calm your anxiety and distract you from cravings during meetings.

I could try that. But it's hard to focus on deep breathing when people are yelling at you about deadlines. Do you think there's any way to deal with that part of it, the meetings and the stress from the other people?

You feel like stress from others, like during meetings, makes it really hard to focus on deep breathing. Can you think of a specific way to take care of yourself in those situations, like taking a few deep breaths before you walk into the meeting, to help you feel more centered and in control?

I guess I could take a few deep breaths before the meeting, but what if it's a super important one and I have to go right in? And what if I have to make a presentation? My anxiety is going to be through the roof. This is all just a lot to think about.

It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed thinking about all the different situations where you might need to manage your anxiety and cravings. Can you imagine practicing those deep breaths in a low-stakes moment, like when you're waiting for a meeting to start, so you feel more confident and prepared for when you actually need them?

Yeah, I could try taking those deep breaths when I'm waiting in the conference room before a meeting. It's not as high-pressure as being in the meeting itself. Maybe if I practice it enough, it'll start to feel more natural and I'll be able to do it even when things get tough.

That's a good start, and it sounds like you're feeling more hopeful about being able to manage your anxiety and cravings. Can you think of a physical activity that you've enjoyed in the past, like drawing or using a stress ball, to keep your hands busy during meetings when you might normally reach for a cigarette?

I used to doodle a lot in meetings, before everyone started having meetings with their laptops open. I liked drawing the weird architectural designs on the conference room walls. Maybe I could pick up drawing again, or even bring a stress ball to the meeting. It's not the same as quitting, but it's something, right?

It sounds like doodling again might be a great way to keep your hands busy in meetings. Would you be open to trying to draw something specific, like the architectural designs, as a way to distract yourself from cravings when you feel stressed or anxious?

Yeah, I could try drawing those designs. But what about when I'm in a meeting and I need to focus on what someone is saying? I don't want to be sketching the ceiling while they're talking about deadlines. That wouldn't be very professional.

You're worried about being able to draw discreetly in meetings, that it might be distracting or unprofessional. Can you think of a way to incorporate drawing into your daily routine, like during breaks or right after meetings, when you have more control over your environment?

I could try drawing during my lunch break or right after a meeting, when I'm waiting for a coffee or something. That way I can focus on my drawing without distracting anyone. And maybe it'll help me unwind after a stressful meeting too.

You're thinking of incorporating drawing into your daily routine in a way that works for you. Now, how might you hold yourself accountable to actually draw during these breaks, so it becomes a consistent habit?

I could set reminders on my phone to remind me to draw during my breaks, or leave a sketchbook on my desk so it's right there when I need it. Maybe I could even set a goal to draw for a certain amount of time each day, like 10 minutes, and reward myself if I stick to it.

You're thinking of setting reminders and goals to make drawing a consistent habit, and even rewarding yourself for sticking to it. Can you imagine how you'll feel when you reach your first milestone, like a week of consistently drawing during your breaks?
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
You're mentioning anxiety again - can you tell me more about what happens to your anxiety when you smoke, and how it makes you feel before you reach for a cigarette?

It's hard to explain... but when I have an anxiety episode, my heart starts racing and everything feels overwhelming. It's like my mind is a million miles an hour and I just can't catch my breath. Before I smoke, I feel like I'm going to have a panic attack. I know it's not a healthy reason to smoke, but it feels like the only thing that helps calm me down, even if it's just for a little while.
It sounds like smoking is closely tied to managing your anxiety episodes. Can you estimate how many cigarettes you typically smoke in a day, and how long you've been smoking?

I'd say I smoke around 8 or 10 cigarettes a day. And... I've been smoking for about 5 years now. It's just become a part of my routine, I guess.  But like I said, I'm not really looking to quit or anything... I'm just here because my therapist said I should address it.

You've been smoking for 5 years, and it's become a part of your daily routine. Can you imagine what your day would be like if you didn't have a cigarette after one of your anxiety episodes, and how you might manage your anxiety in a different way?

Honestly, I don't think I could do it. I mean, I tried to cut back once before, and it was a nightmare. My anxiety got so bad that I couldn't leave my house for days. I don't know... I feel like I'm already so behind compared to my friends, who all smoke and seem totally fine. I don't know if I'm ready to deal with all that on my own.

I can see how difficult it must be for you to consider quitting, especially with the anxiety you've experienced in the past. You've tried reducing your smoking before and ended up feeling overwhelmed. Have you ever thought about what would happen if you didn't reach for a cigarette during an anxiety episode, but instead waited a few minutes before acting on it?

I don't know... I guess that's kind of what my therapist said before, but it's just hard to think about waiting it out. I feel like I'm already running out of time, you know? My anxiety is like this constant thing in the back of my mind, and I'm always worried that if I don't smoke, things are going to spiral out of control. But... I did have one day not too long ago where I was in a meeting and I started to feel anxious, and I just waited it out and it wasn't as bad as I thought it would be. It was actually kind of okay.

It sounds like you had a small victory with waiting it out during that meeting, and that's a great start. Can you think of a way to apply that same idea to your daily life, perhaps by setting a small goal to wait 10 minutes before reaching for a cigarette during an anxiety episode?

I mean, I could try that, I guess. It's not like it's a huge leap, right? Just waiting 10 minutes feels doable, but at the same time... it feels like I'm already setting myself up for failure. What if I'm still feeling anxious after 10 minutes? What if it's even worse? But... I also feel like if I can just get through that first 10 minutes, maybe I can start thinking more clearly and figure out how to deal with it without smoking. Yeah, maybe that's worth a shot.

You're considering trying the 10-minute waiting period, which is a great start. Have you thought about what you could do with those 10 minutes to distract yourself from the craving and anxiety, something that might help make the time feel more manageable?

I think taking a few deep breaths and going for a short walk might help. I've tried meditation before, but it's hard for me to focus. And just focusing on my breathing might actually be kind of calming. I don't know, it's not like I've really thought about it before, but maybe that's something I could try. Do you think that sounds like a good idea?

You've mentioned that taking a few deep breaths and going for a short walk might be helpful during those 10 minutes. Have you ever tried taking a short walk as soon as you feel an anxiety episode coming on, rather than waiting until after the anxiety has kicked in?

Yeah, actually... my partner does that sometimes, and it seems to really help her. She'll just get up and take a quick walk around the block, and it seems to calm her down. Maybe I could try that too... it's not like I'm a complete stranger to walking, but I guess I never thought to do it when I'm feeling anxious.

It sounds like your partner's experience with walking when anxious is helpful to you. Can you imagine yourself taking a quick walk around the block as soon as you start feeling anxious, rather than waiting until the anxiety has intensified?

I... I think I can try that. It's not a huge change, but at least it's a new habit to form. And if it works, it's one less reason to smoke. I mean, that's the goal, right? So... yeah, let's say I'm going to try taking a walk instead of smoking when I feel anxious.
You've taken a brave step towards creating a new, healthier habit, and I'm confident you'll find support within yourself to carry it forward.
I... thank you. Just talking about this stuff is helping me feel a little more hopeful. Maybe I can actually do this. Maybe I don't have to be a smoker forever.
Now that you're feeling more hopeful, can you imagine rewarding yourself with something special after a week of staying smoke-free, just to celebrate that milestone and reinforce your progress?

That's a nice idea... I think. I mean, I'm not great at rewarding myself, but if I'm being honest, it would be really nice to have something to look forward to. Maybe I could treat myself to a massage or something? I've been wanting to try out that new yoga studio too. Yeah, that sounds like a good idea.

You're considering rewarding yourself with a special treat, like a massage or trying out the new yoga studio. What do you think would make trying the new yoga studio even more appealing to you, now that you're thinking about quitting smoking?

I think if I had someone to go with me, that would make it more fun. Maybe my partner could join me, or one of my friends who doesn't smoke. That way it wouldn't feel so lonely, and I'd have someone to talk to afterwards. Yeah, that would be nice.

You've started thinking about how having support from a friend or partner could make trying the new yoga studio more enjoyable. Now, let me ask: Who do you think would be the most supportive person to have by your side when you first start trying to quit smoking?
I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.
It sounds like social situations, especially with friends and in party settings, can be challenging for you. What was going through your mind when your friend offered you a cigarette at the party?
Honestly, it felt like a no-brainer at the time. I mean, it's what we do together, you know? It's part of the group thing. I wasn't thinking too much about it, I just grabbed one.

It sounds like you felt a strong sense of belonging to the group and wanting to fit in when your friend offered you a cigarette. What do you think you would have chosen to do in that moment if you hadn't been smoking?
I think I would've just said something like, "Nah, I'm good, I'm on a break." But then again, it's not that easy, is it?

It sounds like you're recognizing that saying no to your friend wouldn't have been that difficult, but it's the underlying feelings of belonging and wanting to fit in that made it harder for you. What do you think drives that need to fit in and belong in social situations?

I guess it's just who I've been around my whole life. My family, friends, we've all smoked together. It's just part of the culture, you know? And it feels... normal. Like, if I'm not smoking, I'm not really part of the group.

It sounds like you feel like smoking is deeply ingrained in your social culture. Can you think of one person in your life who doesn't smoke, but whom you're close to and might be able to count on for support if you need it?
There's my sister. She's not a smoker, and we're really close. She's actually been trying to get me to quit for years. She's always been supportive, even when I went back to smoking.

It sounds like your sister is someone who already cares about your smoking journey and is willing to support you. Would you be open to talking to her about your quit plan and asking if she'd be willing to be a support person for you in social situations?

Yeah, I think that's a good idea. It would be nice to have her by my side. But I don't know, it's not like I can just tell her to quit smoking for me too, right? I mean, it's hard enough for me to do it myself.

You're recognizing that you can't control your sister's choices, but you can control how you respond to social situations. Would you be willing to ask your sister to be a "quit buddy" of sorts, someone you can call or text when you're in a situation where you feel the urge to smoke?

That sounds like a good way to do it. I can ask her to be my support person without putting any pressure on her. Just someone to text and say, "Hey, I'm at a party and I'm thinking about smoking, can you remind me why I don't want to?"

That's a great attitude to have about involving your sister in your quit journey. Now, have you ever thought about how you'll respond when your sister offers you a cigarette or reminds you about your quit plan in a social situation?

I guess I haven't really thought that far ahead. I mean, I know I'll try to say no, but what if she says something like, "Oh, come on, just one won't hurt"? Or if my friends ask me why I'm not smoking, I don't want to be like, "Oh, I'm trying to quit." I don't know, it just feels weird to say no without giving some kind of reason.

It sounds like you're worried about feeling awkward or defensive when saying no to a cigarette or explaining your quit plan to friends. Would you be open to practicing a simple response like "I'm trying to quit for my health" or "I'm not smoking anymore, thanks for understanding"?
That doesn't sound too bad, I guess. I mean, it's straightforward and it's true. Maybe I can practice saying something like that in the mirror or something.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about saying no to a cigarette or explaining your quit plan to friends. Would you like to actually try practicing that simple response in the mirror right now, so it feels more natural to you?
Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I don't have to do it out loud or anything, right? Just pretend. Okay, let me try it... "I'm trying to quit for my health." Yeah, that feels okay, I guess.

You've taken a positive step by practicing the simple response in the mirror. How do you think you'll feel if you encounter a situation where someone asks you to join them in smoking and you confidently say no using the response you just practiced?

It's hard to say, but I think I'll feel... good, I guess. Like I'm in control of my own actions and I'm not trying to sneak around. It's a weird feeling, because I'm used to feeling like a smoker, you know? But I think it could be kind of liberating to be able to say no and mean it.

You've gained a sense of confidence in saying no to a cigarette, and that's a huge step. Now, how do you think you'll handle those morning cravings, which you mentioned earlier as a trigger, now that you have a simple response to say no to a cigarette?

To be honest, I'm not sure. I know the morning cravings are usually the worst, and they're hard to fight. But if I can get through the morning, I think the rest of the day will be a lot easier. And maybe if I have my sister to text or something, it'll help me get through those tough moments.

You're feeling uncertain about handling those morning cravings. What do you think about making a simple plan for the morning, like drinking a glass of water as soon as you wake up, to help distract you from the craving?
I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

That must be hard for you to feel like you're not making progress with quitting. What was it like for you during those 3 months when you were able to stay quit - was it easier in some ways, or was it just as hard as when you're actively smoking?

During those 3 months, I felt a little better, I guess. My breathing was a bit easier, and I didn't cough as much, but the cravings were still there. It just got to the point where I got tired of fighting it, and I started smoking again. When I'm not smoking, I feel so anxious, especially when I'm driving or in meetings. Those are my worst times.
It sounds like those 3 months were a bit of a mixed bag for you. How many cigarettes do you think you were smoking per day when you finally started smoking again after the 3 months?
I don't know... 15, I guess. Maybe a little more. It's hard to remember, but it wasn't like I was smoking any less when I quit. It was just... easier.

So you're not sure about the exact number of cigarettes you were smoking per day, but you mentioned it wasn't like you were smoking any less when you quit. Did you find that the ease of quitting made you feel like you were in control of your smoking at that time?

I think so. Yeah, I felt like I was in control when I was quit for 3 months. It was like I had won something, you know? But when I started smoking again, it was like I lost all that progress. My husband will sometimes smoke in the house, and it gets to me. He doesn't even realize it.

It sounds like having control over your smoking is an important feeling for you, and your husband's smoking can be a trigger. Can you think of ways to communicate your boundaries with your husband, like asking him to smoke outside or in a different room, rather than smoking in the house?

That sounds easy to say, but it's hard to ask him to do that. He doesn't think it's a big deal, and I don't want to be a nag or ruin our relationship. But maybe if I explain it in a different way... he's always saying I'm his support system, but I'm not really supporting myself right now, am I?

It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck on how to ask your husband to change his behavior in a way that works for both of you. You've also touched on the idea that your relationship with your husband is closely tied to your own self-care and well-being. Have you considered talking to your husband about your own feelings and needs, rather than just asking him to change his behavior?

I don't know... I feel like he'll just tell me I'm overreacting or that it's no big deal. He doesn't understand why it bothers me so much. I've tried talking to him before, and it just ends up in an argument.

It sounds like you're worried that talking to your husband will lead to another argument, and that's making it hard for you to ask for what you need. Are you open to exploring other ways to communicate your needs that might help prevent an argument?
I guess that would be better than just shutting down and not talking about it. But what if it still ends in an argument? I don't know...

It sounds like you're worried about the outcome of the conversation with your husband. Can you think of a way to have this conversation without having to predict the exact outcome, but instead focus on expressing your own needs and feelings?

I'd have to take a deep breath and not get defensive, I guess. Maybe I could say something like, "Hey, I appreciate you smoking, but it's really hard for me when you do it in the house. Could we find a way to do it outside or somewhere else?" Instead of saying, "You need to stop smoking in the house."

You're starting to think about how to express your needs in a way that feels more effective and less likely to lead to an argument. Now that you've thought about how to communicate with your husband, let's think about what you can do when you're driving and feel the urge to smoke, since that's one of your known triggers - how do you think you could take care of yourself in that situation?

I usually try to distract myself by singing along to the radio or something, but that doesn't always work. Sometimes I'll roll down the window and take some deep breaths, but it's hard to get any fresh air that way. I think what I really need is something to do with my hands... like a stress ball or something.

You're acknowledging that distractions like singing haven't been helping as much as you'd like. Have you ever thought about carrying a small notebook or a pen with you in the car, so you can draw or doodle when you feel the urge to smoke?

That's a good idea. I like to draw, and it can be calming. But I don't know if it would work in the car, since I won't be able to see what I'm doing very well. Maybe I could try to do some simple doodles in my head, or just concentrate on the lines and patterns on the steering wheel?

You're thinking creatively about how to adapt your drawing habit to the car. Would you be willing to try carrying a small, portable notebook that you can hold on your lap, so you can still draw while keeping your eyes on the road?

Yeah, that might work. I could use one of those little spiral notebooks and just draw little doodles on the side of the page. And if I need to draw something more specific, like a picture, I could use the dashboard light to help me see what I'm doing.
That sounds like a great plan, using a small notebook to draw while driving. Are you feeling more confident about trying this approach when you feel the urge to smoke while driving?
I think so. I mean, it's not going to be easy, but it sounds like a good place to start. And it's something I can control, which is what's been missing for me.

You sound more confident about trying the notebook approach, which is great. Now, how do you think you can apply this same idea to dealing with meetings and social situations where you might feel the urge to smoke?
I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.
I can imagine how worried you must be about the white patch and the doctor's concerns. When you're feeling stressed or anxious, do you find yourself reaching for gutkha more often?
Yes, I do. When I'm stuck in traffic or have long breaks between rides, I feel like I need to use it to calm down. Sometimes even after meals, I crave it.

It sounds like stressful situations like stuck in traffic or long breaks are triggers for your gutkha use. Would you be open to taking a short walk during these times to see if it helps reduce your cravings and anxiety?

Walking seems okay, but I don't know if it will replace the habit. I mean, while I'm waiting for passengers, my hands just sit there. It's like my body is used to doing something. I don't know what else to do with my hands.

It sounds like you're concerned about replacing the habit of having something to do with your hands while waiting. Would keeping your hands busy with small activities like doodling or using a stress ball during those times help distract you from cravings?

That's not a bad idea. I used to doodle in school, it was fun. Maybe I can take a small notebook and pencil with me, and use it when I'm waiting for passengers. But what if I get cravings at other times, not just when I'm waiting?

You're thinking ahead and considering how you'll handle cravings in different situations. What do you think it would be like to call or text a friend or family member when you're experiencing a craving, just to talk about it and see if they can offer any support?

It's not something I've ever thought of doing before. I usually just eat or chew gutkha when I'm bored or stressed. Talking to someone might actually help, though. It's just that it feels weird to share something like this with others, you know? It's like, they don't understand how much gutkha is just... part of my life.

You're worried that others won't understand how deeply gutkha is ingrained in your life. How do you think I could help you find someone to talk to who might get it, someone who's been through a similar experience?

A friend of mine's brother used to be a heavy gutkha user too, and he quit a few years ago. I never talked to him about it, but maybe I could ask him now. Or there's a guy who drives for the same company as me, he's always talking about how he's trying to quit. Maybe he'd be willing to talk to me too.

You're considering reaching out to your friend's brother and the coworker who's also trying to quit. What do you think you could ask them about their experience of quitting, that would help you feel more confident and prepared to tackle your own challenges?

I could ask them about their hardest moments, how they handled cravings and triggers. Maybe they could give me some tips on what helped them stay on track, and what didn't work. I'd also like to know how long it took them to completely quit, and how their lives changed afterwards.

You're taking a thoughtful approach to understanding their experiences and gaining valuable insights. What if, in addition to asking them about their strategies, you also asked them to write down a few reasons why quitting gutkha is so important to you, and what specific benefits you hope to achieve by quitting?

That's a good idea. Writing it down might make me think more clearly about why I want to quit. I've been thinking about my health, obviously, because of the white patch and the doctor's warning. But I've also been thinking about my savings, I spend a lot of money on gutkha every day. It's around 150 rupees, that's a lot of money for little pouches.

You've started to clarify your reasons for quitting, and it's helpful that you're thinking about the financial impact of your gutkha habit. Have you ever tried keeping a daily log of how much you spend on gutkha, and how it affects your finances?

No, I don't think so. I just know that I spend a lot on it. But now that you mention it, I think that would be a good idea. Knowing exactly how much I'm spending every day might make it feel more real, you know? Make me think about it more.

You think keeping a daily log of your gutkha expenses might help you see its impact more clearly. Now, would you be willing to also write down each time you spend money on gutkha, to see if there are any patterns or situations that tend to trigger your purchases?

Yeah, that sounds like a good idea too. I can try to write it down, see when I usually buy gutkha. Maybe it's just when I stop for a meal or at the traffic light, like I said before. Writing it down might help me see those patterns more clearly.

You think writing it down might help you see those patterns more clearly. Would you be willing to try keeping a log of not just when you buy gutkha, but also what you do immediately before buying it, so we can see if there's something specific that's leading to those purchases?

I can try. It might be a bit more work, but if it helps me understand why I buy it, then it's worth it. It's just... sometimes I'm not really thinking about it, I just do it. But if I can figure out what's behind it, maybe I can break that habit.

It sounds like you're feeling hopeful about breaking this habit. Would you be willing to try setting a small goal for yourself, like going one day without buying gutkha, to see if you can make progress on your own before we explore more strategies together?

Yeah, I think I can do that. One day without gutkha sounds manageable. It's just one day, I can make it through that. And if I can make it through one day, maybe it's not so hard to make it through another...

You feel like one day without gutkha is a manageable goal. What do you think about taking a short walk after meals or when you feel the urge to buy gutkha, to see if it helps distract you from cravings?
I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.

It sounds like smoking became a part of your college experience with friends, and now you're concerned about how it's affecting your stamina on the cricket team. Can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you're on the cricket field and feeling tired - do you feel like you need a cigarette to perform better?

Yeah, like, when I'm out there and feeling tired, I just think I need something to clear my head, you know? It's like my friends, they all do it before the game, so it feels like it should be helping, but... cricket coach says I'm losing my spot on the team, and that's really bothering me.

It sounds like you're feeling pressured to perform well on the cricket team and worried about losing your spot, which is making you reach for cigarettes when you're tired. Have you ever tried taking a short walk or doing some light physical activity to see if that helps clear your head and boost your energy instead of smoking?
Not really... I mean, I've thought about it, but it's just easier to grab a cigarette, you know? And my friends, they all do it, so it feels normal. I don't want to be the one who can't keep up.

It sounds like you're feeling stuck in a habit that's hard to break, especially with your friends around. Can you imagine having someone in your life who's actively supportive of your quit attempt, like a friend or family member who could text you or call when you're feeling the urge to smoke?

I don't know, like, my parents would freak out if they found out I smoke... I don't want to tell them. And I'm not really close to my family about this kind of stuff. But my coach is really supportive about quitting, he says it'll help my game... maybe I could talk to him about it?

It sounds like your coach's support is really resonating with you and making you consider talking to him about your quit attempt. Have you thought about how you might feel if your coach were to accompany you to social situations where you might otherwise be tempted to smoke?
Yeah, that's a good idea, I guess. Having him there would make it feel like less of a big deal, you know? But what if my friends are like "what's the catch?" or something?

You're feeling a bit concerned about how your friends might react if you're with your coach at social situations. Can you imagine how your coach might respond if your friends do ask "what's the catch"?

He'd probably just tell them that it's good for my game, and that I'm trying to quit for health reasons. He's all about sportsmanship and being a team player... maybe they'd actually respect him for it.

It sounds like your coach's support and values are really resonating with you, and that he might be a great ally in your quit attempt. Would you be open to having a conversation with your coach about how he could specifically support you in social situations where you're tempted to smoke?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think talking to him about it would be a good idea, see what he's willing to do. But what if he's busy, or... or he can't make it to every situation?

It sounds like you're worried about having a backup plan in case your coach can't be there. Can you think of someone else who might be able to provide support for you in those situations, like a family member or another teammate?

There's this one teammate, Rohan, he's always been a bit quieter than the rest of us, but he's always been supportive with me. He's never smoked, so maybe he could be someone to talk to if I'm feeling tempted.

It sounds like you've identified a potential ally in Rohan, and I think it's great that you're considering reaching out to him for support. Would you be comfortable asking Rohan if he'd be willing to be a "quit buddy" and check in with you regularly to see how you're doing?
Yeah, I think I could ask him that. He's always been pretty reliable, so I don't think he'd flake on me. But what if he's not serious about quitting or something?

Let's consider the possibility that Rohan might not be a good fit as a quit buddy, but that doesn't mean you can't find someone else who is. What would you do if you asked Rohan and he wasn't interested in being a quit buddy?
I guess I'd just keep looking, see who else might be willing to help. There are other teammates I get along with, maybe one of them would be interested.

You're open to finding a quit buddy and willing to consider different team members for support. What if your quit buddy is just someone to talk to when you're feeling stressed or tempted, rather than someone who has to be a perfect match in terms of quitting goals or experience?
That makes sense, I guess. It's not like I'm looking for someone to quit with, I just need someone to talk to when I'm feeling stuck.

It sounds like you're feeling more open to the idea of having someone to reach out to when you're feeling stressed or tempted. Would you be willing to ask Rohan if he'd be willing to have a conversation with you when you're struggling with those feelings, even if he's not quitting himself?
Yeah, I can ask him that. It's not a huge ask, just having someone to listen I think would help.

You're feeling more hopeful about having someone to talk to when you're struggling. What would you say to Rohan if you asked him to be a listening ear, rather than a quit buddy, to see if he's still willing to support you?
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.

It sounds like you've been smoking bidis for 30 years and feeling a bit uncertain about quitting now. What are some of the moments or situations when you find yourself reaching for a bidi, like after a meal or when you're feeling stressed?
After I finish cooking, I usually smoke a bidi. It's a habit, I guess. Helps me relax.

It sounds like cooking and eating meals have become a trigger for you to reach for a bidi, and you've been using it as a way to unwind and relax after a meal. How do you think your life would change if you were able to break this habit and find other ways to calm down after cooking?
I don't know... my hands feel empty after eating. It's a habit, like I said. I'll just have one bidi, what's the harm? My son's always saying something, but...

It sounds like you're feeling a strong sense of habit and it's hard to imagine breaking it. Would you be willing to try something small, like having a glass of water after you finish cooking, to see if it can help fill that empty feeling in your hands?
Maybe. Water's okay, I guess. But it's not the same as having a bidi.

You're a bit hesitant about trying water as an alternative, but you're open to it. Would you be willing to try saying something out loud to yourself, like "I've finished cooking, I don't need to smoke," when you feel the urge to reach for a bidi after eating?
I don't know... that sounds a bit silly. But I'll try. Can I say it in my own language, Marathi? "Khavli purni, bidi cheez nahi?"
I appreciate your willingness to try something new, and I'll be here to support you as you work through this process.
Thank you. But I have to think about my afternoons too. I rest a lot in the afternoons and smoke paan. That's a big habit. I don't know if I can quit all of it at once.

It sounds like afternoon rest is a challenging time for you, and paan has become a big part of that routine. Would you be willing to try setting a small goal, like getting through one afternoon rest without paan, to start breaking that habit down into smaller steps?
One afternoon without paan... that's a start, I suppose. Can I have a replacement in case I get cravings, like a sweet or something?
You're feeling more hopeful about taking small steps towards quitting paan. What kind of sweet or replacement would you like to have ready to go when those afternoon cravings strike?
Maybe some jalebi or a small piece of fruit. My grandson loves fruits, I'll try it for him.

It sounds like you're feeling more positive about having some healthier options ready to help you through those afternoon cravings. Would you be willing to try keeping a small stash of your chosen fruits or jalebi at home, so it's easily accessible when you need it?
Yes, I can do that. My son can help me get some fruits from the market. That way, it'll be ready when I need it.

You're feeling more in control of having healthy options ready, and your son's help will definitely make a difference. What specific times during the afternoon do you usually feel the strongest urge to have paan?
It's usually after I take my nap, around 2-3 in the afternoon. That's when I feel like having paan.

You're becoming more aware of the specific time when your cravings for paan are strongest. Now, let's try a strategy that might help you ride out those cravings - when you feel the urge to have paan after your nap, would you be willing to give yourself a 10-minute delay before acting on it, and see if you can find something else to focus on during that time?
10 minutes? That's doable. I can listen to some Bhajan music, or read a prayer from the Bhagavad Gita. That might help.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about trying out the 10-minute delay strategy. Would you be willing to make a mental note of what you're doing during those 10 minutes, like listening to music or reading, so it becomes a consistent habit to help distract you from the craving?
I'll do that. I'll listen to Dholki music, it's soothing and I like it. Maybe I'll even sing along.
It's wonderful to see you've found a personal touch that will help make this strategy feel more enjoyable and sustainable for you.
I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

It sounds like your wife's request is a big motivator for you to quit, but it's also clear that weekend drinking can trigger a lot of smoking for you. Have you ever thought about how you could find other ways to cope with those cravings when you're drinking on weekends?

I've tried, but it's hard. When I'm drinking, I just want to smoke, you know? It feels like they go together. I don't know, maybe I could try gum or something, but I've heard that's not always effective.

It sounds like you feel a strong connection between drinking and smoking, and that can make it tough to break the habit. Have you considered trying to delay your smoking by just a few minutes when you feel the urge while drinking, to see if that helps you break the association between the two behaviors?

Delaying it by a few minutes? That might work, I guess. But it's not like I can avoid drinking altogether; I've got friends I see on the weekends and we like to have a few beers. Can't just quit doing that.

It sounds like you're worried about giving up socializing with your friends on weekends, but have you thought about whether there's a way you could still spend time with them without drinking as much?

I don't know, it's a hassle. We do it together because we like to. But maybe... maybe I could just have a couple of beers and not smoke as much. Is that like, a step in the right direction or something?

It sounds like you're feeling a little uncertain about giving up your weekend socializing, but at the same time, you're willing to try making some changes. Can you imagine what your life might be like on a weekend without smoking at all?

Yeah... I don't know. It's weird to think about. I mean, I'll probably still hang out with my friends, but it'll just be a little different. I might feel weird, I don't know. And I'm sure my wife will be happy, that's a plus, I guess.

You're starting to imagine a life without smoking on weekends, and that's a great start. Considering your friends will still be there, but you'll be doing things differently, have you thought about writing down some ideas for alternative activities you could do on weekends to replace smoking?

I could, I guess. Like what, though? I usually just sit around and shoot the breeze with them. We could play some cards or something, I don't know. Or maybe just go for a walk or something. Is that enough to actually help, or is it just going to be the same old thing?

You're thinking about playing cards or going for a walk as alternatives to smoking - that's a good start. Would it be helpful to try doing a short walk together with your friends on your next weekend outing, just to see how it goes?

That sounds like a good idea, I think. Yeah, we could try that. I just don't know if it'll actually stick. I mean, if I'm feeling stressed or whatever, I'll probably just want to sit down and smoke anyway. But at least it's something to try, right?

You're feeling uncertain about whether this will actually help you quit smoking, and you're worried that stress might still lead you to reach for a cigarette. Would it be helpful to try a small exercise to calm your body when you feel stressed, like taking a few deep breaths together in this session?
Deep breaths, yeah, that's not a bad idea. I guess it's worth trying, anyway. Just to see if it actually works or not. Go ahead, what do I do?

Let's give deep breathing a try. To start, just breathe in slowly through your nose, hold it for a second, and then exhale slowly through your mouth. Can you feel your body begin to relax a bit as you take that first deep breath?
Yeah... I feel a little calmer, I guess. It's weird, my shoulders feel like they're relaxing or something. I didn't even think about them being tense.

You're feeling a bit calmer and noticing physical relaxation, which is great. Now, let's talk about how you can incorporate this feeling into your life when you're feeling stressed or reaching for a cigarette - do you think you could take a few deep breaths before making a decision to smoke?

Yeah, that's a good point. If I'm feeling stressed, maybe I could just take a couple of deep breaths first and see how I feel. It might not be easy, but if it helps me quit smoking, it's worth trying. Okay, I think I'm starting to get this. What comes next?
