You're feeling more hopeful about using deep breathing as a tool to manage stress and reduce your urge to smoke. Now, would you be open to trying a small activity to keep your hands busy when you feel the urge to smoke, like carrying a stress ball or a small notebook to doodle in?

A stress ball or a notebook sounds like something I could do. I already doodle sometimes, so it's not like it's a big change. I could carry a stress ball with me on the weekends, just in case. That might actually help.

You're feeling more hopeful about using a stress ball or notebook to keep your hands busy, which could really help when you're feeling stressed or reaching for a cigarette. Do you think you'd be willing to try carrying one of these with you every day, not just on the weekends?

Yeah, I guess I could do that. It's not like it's going to be a big hassle or anything. I already carry my phone and my wallet with me, so a stress ball or notebook would just be one more thing. Okay, I'm willing to try it.
You're taking a thoughtful step towards making a positive change, and I'm wishing you all the best in your ongoing journey.
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.

It sounds like the thought of having a baby is a big motivator for you to quit smoking. What are some situations or emotions that you think might make it harder for you to resist smoking in the next few months, now that you're planning to start a family?

Work breaks, that's a big one. I always seem to grab a cigarette when I'm stressed, and teaching can be pretty stressful at times. And then, I guess, dinner with family or friends will be different, too.

It sounds like work breaks and stress are significant challenges for you, and I'm glad you're aware of how teaching can be stressful. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside during your work breaks to see if it helps you manage stress and reduces the urge to smoke?
I've tried walking, yeah, but it's not always easy. Sometimes I feel like I'm too anxious to get up and walk around, and even when I do, I'm still thinking about smoking.

It sounds like walking hasn't been as helpful as you'd hoped, especially when you're feeling anxious. Can you tell me more about what happens for you when you feel anxious - is it a racing mind, physical tension, or something else?

It's kind of a combination of both, I guess. My mind starts racing, and I feel a little jittery inside. Sometimes I get a knot in my stomach, but I think that's more when I'm worried about something specific. When it's just anxiety, it's more like... I don't know, a feeling of being on edge, I suppose.

It sounds like your mind starts racing and your body gets a little tense when you're feeling anxious. Have you ever tried deep breathing exercises to calm yourself down and see if it helps reduce those feelings of anxiety and the urge to smoke?
Not really, no. I mean, I've heard people talk about it, but I've never actually tried it myself. I'm not sure if it would work for me, but I'm willing to give it a shot, I guess.
That takes a lot of courage to consider trying something new. Would you be open to trying some deep breathing exercises during our next session, and I can guide you through them to see how it feels?
I'd be open to it, yeah. I just want to make sure I can handle quitting before I get pregnant. I don't want to have to go through this again if we're not successful.

It sounds like you're thinking about the best time to quit for your family planning goals. Are you willing to consider quitting for a period of time before you're pregnant to test your ability to stick to a quit plan?

I mean, it's not ideal because we want to have a baby soon, but if it means I can stop smoking for good, then yeah, that makes sense. Can you give me an idea of when I should start trying to quit, and how long I should stick to it?

You're considering quitting to test your ability to stick to a quit plan before having a baby. Can you imagine what your daily life would be like if you successfully quit smoking for a period of time, and what would that say to you about your ability to do it for good?

If I can do it for a few months, it would mean a lot to me. I'd feel like I can really do it, you know? It would be a big confidence booster, and I think it would make a big difference in our decision to have a baby.

It sounds like quitting for a few months would be a significant milestone for you, and it would give you the confidence to take the next step towards having a baby. Can you think of a small reward you could give yourself after quitting for a certain amount of time, like a week or two, to help motivate you to stay on track?
I could do something fun with my husband, like take a weekend trip or try a new restaurant. Or maybe I could buy a new outfit or something. Having something to look forward to would be nice.

You're excited about the idea of rewarding yourself for quitting milestones, like a weekend trip or new outfit. Would you be willing to try keeping a small reward journal, where you write down the reward you'd like to give yourself each time you reach a quit milestone?
That sounds like a good idea. I like the idea of writing it down, so it feels more official, you know? Yeah, I'd be willing to do that.
That's great, you're open to the idea of a reward journal. How do you think you could make it a habit to write in the journal each day, so you can track your progress and stay motivated?
I guess I could put it on my phone, so it's always with me, and remind myself to write in it every day. Maybe I could even set a reminder, so it's like a little accountability system for myself.

You seem to be on board with using reminders on your phone to write in your reward journal. How do you think you can make writing in the journal a consistent habit, especially on days when you're feeling stressed or anxious, which is when you mentioned cravings tend to be stronger?

I don't know, maybe I could make it a part of my daily routine, like right after I take a sip of my morning coffee or something. That way, it's just automatic, and I don't have to think about it as much.

It sounds like you're thinking of making writing in your reward journal a consistent habit by linking it to a daily routine, like right after your morning coffee. How about incorporating a small physical activity, like taking a few deep breaths or stretching, to help calm your nerves and reduce morning cravings before writing in your journal?
I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

You're working from home now, and that's led to a significant increase in your smoking. I'm curious, what do you think might be different about your daily routine at home that's contributing to your increased smoking?

To be honest, I think it's the constant switching between work and personal tasks. I find myself taking breaks to smoke when I get overwhelmed or need a change of scenery, but there's no actual change of scenery when you're working from home.

It sounds like the lack of a clear separation between work and personal space is contributing to your increased smoking. Can you think of one specific part of your day when you usually take these smoking breaks, like right after a task or a specific time in the morning?

It's usually after I've been stuck on a particular task for a while, I get frustrated and take a break to smoke. And then sometimes I'll use it as a reward to myself after completing a difficult task or at lunchtime, it's like a habitual thing now.

It sounds like frustration is a key trigger for you, and then you often use smoking as a coping mechanism to take a break or reward yourself. Can you imagine trying to delay your response to that frustration by waiting 10 minutes before giving in to the urge to smoke?

I don't know, it sounds easy to say, but actually doing it might be tough. I've tried this before and it didn't work out. I'd say I'm frustrated, wait 10 minutes, and then I'd just be even more frustrated because I still haven't solved the problem. Plus, I'd probably just find some other way to distract myself, like scrolling through my phone or something.

You're right, it can be tough to delay your response to frustration, especially if you're not sure what else to do with that feeling. I'm wondering, what do you think you might do with that extra 10 minutes if you're not smoking?

I'd probably end up browsing social media or checking my emails again, but I don't really want to do that. I've been feeling kinda stuck, like I'm not really accomplishing anything when I'm working from home. Maybe in that 10 minutes, I could do something more productive, like reviewing my task list or breaking down a big project into smaller steps.

It sounds like you're recognizing some potential ways to use those 10 minutes more productively. Would you be open to trying to replace your usual smoking break with a short walk around the house or outside, just to see if that helps shift your focus and reduce your stress?

I've tried taking short walks before, but I'd usually just end up smoking afterwards anyway. But maybe this time, if I'm actually trying to focus on the walk and not smoking, it could be different. What if I, uh, set a goal to walk 300 steps every time I feel frustrated? Would that be a good starting point?

That's an interesting approach with setting a goal to walk 300 steps. You're trying to create a new habit to replace your usual smoking break. Can you imagine setting a timer for 5 minutes during your walk, and using that time to focus on your breathing and clear your mind?

I can try. I've always been interested in mindfulness and meditation, but I've never actually made the time for it. Having a timer and a specific goal might make it more structured and less overwhelming. Does that mean I'll still be smoking at the same rate initially, and then try to eventually phase it out?

That's a great insight into how a structured approach to mindfulness might work for you. If you were to start with those 5-minute breathing exercises during your walks, how do you think it would feel to gradually increase the time to 10 minutes, then 15, and so on?

I think it could help me get more comfortable with the idea of taking a break from my tasks without smoking. It's funny, when I think about it, I've been trying to track my cigarette smoking to try and cut back, but that didn't work out. But the idea of tracking my breathing exercises and gradually increasing the time feels more manageable.

That's a great insight into how tracking your progress might be more effective with a positive focus, like your breathing exercises. Would you be open to writing down or journaling about how you feel after each of these 5-minute breathing exercises, to see if it helps you identify any patterns or areas where you might need extra support?

Yeah, that makes sense. Writing down my feelings might help me see some patterns I'm not even aware of. And if I'm being honest, it's not just about the smoking, it's about feeling more focused and productive when I work from home. If I can figure out how to manage my frustration and boredom, maybe that'll help with the smoking too.

You've shared a really honest reflection about your goals and motivations beyond just quitting smoking. You want to feel more focused, productive, and comfortable with breaks from tasks. Have you noticed any moments or situations where you feel like you're getting close to managing your frustration and boredom, even if it's just for a brief moment?

There was one day last week when I was working on a project and I got stuck on a tricky problem. Instead of smoking, I decided to take a 5-minute walk and practiced my breathing exercises. When I came back, I was able to approach the problem with a fresh perspective and eventually solved it. It was a small win, but it felt good. I realized that I didn't need smoking to take a break and come back to my tasks feeling more focused. Maybe I can build on that.

You've had a small but significant win by finding an alternative to smoking when feeling stuck. It sounds like your breathing exercises and short walk helped you regain focus and solve the problem. Would you be willing to try to recreate that scenario in a situation where you're feeling overwhelmed or frustrated, and see if you can build on this momentum?

Yeah, I feel like I'm starting to see some actual progress here. If I can recreate that scenario, maybe I can develop a new habit of dealing with frustration without smoking. It's not going to be easy, but I think I'm ready to try. What's the next step?
It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge your readiness for change and take that first step, and I'm here to support you every step of the way.

You've had success with incorporating breathing exercises and short walks when feeling stuck. Would you be open to trying a similar approach after meals, when you mentioned morning cravings often arise, and see if it helps you break the automatic link between eating and smoking?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.

It sounds like you've made some progress in cutting down your bidi smoking, which is great. What are some situations or emotions that make you want to smoke 25 bidis a day, and are those same situations or emotions still happening now when you're smoking fewer bidis?

It was the stress of work, I guess. And when I'd get frustrated because I'd forget things, like where I put my keys. Those were the times I'd smoke more. But I don't have that job stress anymore, so...

You're saying that job stress used to trigger your smoking, but now that's not an issue anymore. Does frustration about forgetting things still feel like a strong urge to smoke, or is that feeling less intense for you now?
It's...less intense, yes. I still get frustrated, but I don't crave a bidi as much. I can manage it. But the morning one, the first bidi of the day, that's the hardest. Always has been.

It sounds like the morning bidi craving is still a tough one for you. Have you ever tried doing something physical right after waking up, like taking a short walk, to see if it helps distract you from that craving?
I've tried walking, but it's winter now and the mornings are chilly, so I don't feel like going out. And it's not like I want to exercise, per se, I just want to...you know, smoke.

You're saying that walking didn't work for you in the past, and now it's even less appealing because of the cold weather. Would it be helpful to try something a bit different, like brushing your teeth or drinking a glass of water, as soon as you wake up to see if that helps reduce your craving?
Brushing my teeth, yeah, that's not a bad idea. It's something I need to do anyway. But what if I still want a bidi after brushing my teeth?

You're thinking about what might happen if brushing your teeth doesn't work, which shows you're already anticipating challenges. Can you be honest with me, how likely are you to give in to that craving even after brushing your teeth?

It's...possible. I haven't given in in a while, but it's still a possibility. I mean, I'm not like I'm 10 days into quitting, you know? I've been cutting down for months. It's just this last part. It's hard.

You're acknowledging that there's still a strong possibility of giving in to that craving, even after brushing your teeth. How do you think you'll handle the feeling of frustration if you do end up giving in to that craving?
I'll feel like I've taken a step back, I guess. Frustrated with myself. But I've been trying not to be too hard on myself, just focus on the day after.

You're recognizing that it's okay to take a step back, and that's a big step forward. Can you try to imagine what you would tell a friend who's struggling with quitting, if they were feeling frustrated with themselves for giving in to a craving?
I'd tell them it's okay, that it's just a setback. That they're not defined by one mistake. I'd tell them to be kind to themselves and keep moving forward. Easier said than done, though.

It sounds like you're already thinking like a supportive friend, which is really helpful. Now, let's think about who in your life could be that supportive person for you, someone who knows about your quit goal and will be there to encourage you when you need it.
My wife, maybe. She's been supportive of my trying to quit, but I don't want to bother her all the time. I'll think about asking her, though.

That makes sense, feeling a bit hesitant to bother your wife with your quit struggles. Are you considering sharing your struggles with your wife more openly, like you would with a friend, to see if it helps you feel more supported?
I think I need to. She's been patient with me for so long, I should be more open with her. Maybe it'll help me stay committed to quitting.

You're considering being more open with your wife about your quit struggles, and that's a great step. How do you think she'll react if you ask her to be that supportive person for you, and to be someone you can call or talk to when you're feeling frustrated or tempted to smoke?
I think she'll want to help. She's always been supportive, like I said. And it's not like I'm asking her to quit with me or anything, just to be there for me.

You're feeling hopeful that your wife will be supportive, which is great. If she does offer to be that person for you, how do you think you'll reach out to her when you're feeling the urge to smoke and need some encouragement?
I'll probably just call her or text her, I don't know. Maybe at first, I'll just send her a message saying I'm feeling tempted and could use some reassurance. See how she responds.

That sounds like a good plan to reach out to your wife for support. Before you send that first message, what will you remind yourself about why you're quitting, to help keep you motivated and focused on your goal?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.
You're feeling guilty about the financial burden your smoking is placing on your family - can you tell me more about what triggers you to smoke when you're feeling guilty?

Like when I come back home from a long delivery and I see my kids asking for money for school fees, I want to relax and sometimes I grab a cigarette. And then after meals too, it's a habit, like it's a reward or something.

You're describing a moment where you're feeling overwhelmed by the weight of responsibility and your kids are asking for help, and that's triggering a cigarette. When you're feeling like you need a break after meals, what's the first thing that comes to mind that you do right now, before even thinking about a cigarette?
I go to the kitchen and get a cup of chai. It's just a habit, I guess. But the thing is, chai tastes nice, but I still want to sit and relax, and then I think about having a cigarette.

You're recognizing that getting a cup of chai is a habit for you, and it's triggering a desire to sit and relax, which then leads to thinking about a cigarette. What if, instead of getting chai, you started walking to the living room after meals and doing something else for a few minutes before even thinking about a cigarette, like watering the plants or taking a few deep breaths?

I don't know, it sounds a bit too much effort. I mean, I'm tired after a long day, and walking to the living room just feels like extra work. But, I guess that 4000 rupees a month thing is still bothering me...

I get that you're feeling overwhelmed with the idea of walking after meals when you're already tired. What if, instead of making this a big change, you started by just taking a few extra steps - literally, just a few more steps from the kitchen to the living room, that's all - and see how that feels?
It's not like it's a lot of distance, I guess. Okay, I can do that. Just a few steps, no big deal. Maybe it'll make a difference, I don't know. But 4000 rupees is a lot, so...

You're feeling more open to making a small change in your daily routine, like taking a few extra steps after meals. Does taking a few deep breaths right before you take those extra steps feel like something that might help you distract from the craving for a cigarette?

Deep breathing sounds okay, I guess. I mean, it's not like I hate taking deep breaths or anything. But it's still just a few moments, and then I'm still gonna be wanting a cigarette, right? I'm not sure it's gonna make that much of a difference.

You're feeling a bit skeptical about whether deep breathing will really help with your cravings. What if I told you that a lot of people notice that their cravings for a cigarette start to fade after just 30 seconds to a minute of taking slow, deep breaths?
30 seconds isn't so long, I suppose. But what's the plan after that? Do I just sit there taking deep breaths forever? What's the goal here?

You're thinking about the long-term plan, not just the short-term fix. What if, after taking those 30 seconds of deep breaths, you try to do something else with your hands, like fidgeting with a pen or a rubber band, to distract yourself from the craving?

I can do that. I already fidget with my phone when I'm bored, why not a pen? But what about the smoking itself? Are we just gonna focus on not smoking for those few seconds or do we actually have a plan to cut down or quit for good?

You're feeling like we need a more long-term plan to deal with the smoking itself. What if, as you start fidgeting with a pen, you also think about a specific goal you have for quitting, like being healthier for your family or saving money, to help motivate you to keep moving forward with quitting?

Okay, I can try that. Saving money for my kids' education is a big goal for me. If I could cut down on the cigarettes, I'd have more money for that. But it's gonna be tough, I know I've tried before and I ended up going back to my usual habit.

You're willing to try a new approach, and that's a great first step. Since you've tried quitting before and relapsed, do you think there's anything in particular that led you to go back to smoking that time?

It was a bad traffic jam on the way to a delivery, and I was so stressed out and anxious, and next thing I knew I was lighting up. My boss yelled at me for being late, my car was stuck in traffic, and I just lost my cool. That's when I started smoking again. It was just a really bad day, you know?

It sounds like that traffic jam was a perfect storm of stress and frustration that triggered your smoking. What if, next time you're stuck in a similar situation, you can take a few deep breaths and remind yourself that you're not just trying to cope with that moment, but also taking care of your long-term goal of saving money for your kids' education?

You know, that might actually work. I mean, when I'm stuck in traffic, it's easy to think about just giving up and smoking. But if I can focus on my kids and how much this is for them... maybe I can make it through the day without reaching for a cigarette.
